exemplar.title: MYP Personal Project - Creating Soccer Training and Tactical Videos

detailedExemplarData.title: MYP Personal Project - Creating Soccer Training and Tactical Videos

strengths: Strong ATL research skills evidenced by detailed explanations of source selection and application of the CARRDS method.
Use of primary interviews alongside secondary sources demonstrates rigor and credibility in gathering information.
Adaptability and creative problem-solving are clear in the description of overcoming technical editing challenges.
Professional editing choices and smooth transitions reveal effective use of media-related ATL thinking skills to enhance production quality.
weaknesses: Connections between overcoming editing challenges and specific ATL thinking or self-management strategies are not fully articulated, limiting insight into skill impact.
Future improvement ideas are described at a high level without linking to particular ATL skills like collaboration or communication.
Areas for improvement lack concrete, step-by-step action plans or timelines for skill application and refinement.
feedback.calculationMethod: Mini report holistic evaluation: The student demonstrates robust application of research and thinking ATL skills, particularly in sourcing information and solving technical issues. However, the narrative would benefit from explicit mapping of challenges to specific ATL strategies and clearer plans for applying these skills to future enhancements, ensuring a stronger link between strategy and outcome.

strengths: Compelling personal connection to soccer grounds the learning goal in genuine interest and motivation.
Clear, detailed descriptions of two distinct video products demonstrate thorough consideration of content scope and sequence.
Identification of multiple product dimensions (aesthetics, engagement, accessibility, safety) reflects a holistic approach to planning.
Well-structured project plan with dual timelines and to-do lists shows effective sequencing aligned to objectives.
weaknesses: Success criteria remain high-level and non-measurable, with the criteria table left empty and lacking concrete metrics.
Product-specific timeline omits clear milestones or checkpoints, making progress monitoring difficult.
General timeline phases are not linked to specific resources or deliverables, reducing feasibility and clarity.
Success criteria for each dimension need specific targets (e.g., engagement rate percentages or accessibility feedback thresholds) to enable objective evaluation.
feedback.calculationMethod: Mini report holistic evaluation: The plan is grounded in genuine personal interest and outlines a feasible, multi-angle approach, but it falls short in defining measurable success criteria and detailed milestones. Strengthening the plan with specific metrics, resource mapping and clear checkpoints will transform a promising framework into a fully actionable roadmap.

strengths: Reflection connects personal and academic growth across multiple ATL skills and outlines how these will inform future projects.
Emphasis on experimentation as a learning strategy shows strong metacognitive awareness and willingness to iterate.
Use of the submission review stage to measure outcomes against initial objectives demonstrates disciplined self-evaluation.
Insight into the value of feedback underscores an understanding of iterative improvement and audience-centered reflection.
weaknesses: Evaluation of the product against each success criterion is not broken down individually or supported by specific evidence such as viewer metrics or feedback data.
âAreas for Improvementâ lacks precise action plans, timelines, or responsibility assignments for suggested enhancements.
Discussion of new skill development remains broad and would benefit from concrete examples (e.g., mastering a particular editing function and its effect on pacing).
feedback.calculationMethod: Mini report holistic evaluation: The reflective section shows commendable self-awareness and links learning experiences to future applications, yet it stops short of providing criterion-by-criterion evaluations and actionable next steps. Incorporating detailed evidence and structured action plans will deepen the reflection and guide continued growth.

annotations.comment: The success criteria table is emptyâpopulate it with concrete criteria and metrics to track aesthetics, engagement, accessibility, and safety against your goals.
You effectively explain how you gathered and evaluated information. Detailed examples of source selection show strong ATL research skills.
You establish a compelling personal connection to soccer, grounding your learning goal in genuine passion and experience. This foundation strengthens your project purpose and motivates your planning.
Your description of the two video products is clear and detailed, showing youâve thought through content scope and sequence. This supports feasibility in your planning.
Descriptions of these dimensions remain at a surface level. Define success criteria for each (e.g. â95% positive feedback on accessibilityâ) to make evaluation feasible.
Your project plan clearly describes using two timelines and to-do lists, showing effective sequencing and alignment with success criteria.

Your âProduct success criteriaâ section outlines high-level goals but lacks specific, measurable indicators. Add detailed criteria (e.g. target engagement rate, clarity metrics) to evaluate each video objectively.
Youâve identified key product dimensions (aesthetics, engagement, accessibility, safety), demonstrating holistic planning. This multi-angle approach enhances project relevance.
Product-specific timeline lists activities but omits expected outcomes or checkpoints. Add clear milestones (e.g. draft storyboard complete) to monitor progress.
The general timeline is clear, but consider linking each phase to specific resources and deliverables (e.g. equipment, software versions) to strengthen feasibility.
You note achieving most success criteria, but you could deepen this by evaluating each criterion individually, using evidence such as viewer feedback or data points.
Your personal and academic growth reflection covers multiple ATL skills and shows broad impact, linking experiences to future application effectively.
âAreas for Improvementâ identifies useful ideas but lacks specific action plans. Specify how and when youâll implement interactive content or formatting consistency.
Your use of the CARRDS method and inclusion of primary interviews and secondary sources demonstrates rigorous research. This strengthens the credibility of your content.
You describe overcoming editing challenges, but you could more explicitly link ATL thinking or self-management strategies to how improvements impacted your product quality.
You illustrate critical and creative thinking when solving technical issues in editing. This shows adaptability and effective application of ATL thinking skills.
In âAreas for Improvement,â you identify high-level ideas but stop short of linking specific ATL skills (e.g. collaboration, communication) to future enhancements of your product.
Highlighting professional editing and smooth transitions demonstrates your effective use of media-related ATL skills in enhancing production quality.
Your reflection on learning emphasizes experimentation as a key learning strategy, providing clear evidence of growth and metacognitive awareness.
You use the submission review stage to measure success against initial objectives, demonstrating strong reflective practice and self-evaluation.
Your coverage of both skills and tactics shows strong application of ATL thinking skills to ensure content completeness and relevance for diverse audiences.
Your insight that feedback is key highlights your understanding of iterative improvement and audience-centered reflection.
Your discussion of skill development lists broad gains but could be enhanced by concrete examples (e.g. âlearned X function in CapCut, leading to Y improvement in video pacingâ).
